thread.title: UNAIR and Oxford University Collaborate on Comparative Lecture on China’s Marriage Law
thread.title_full: UNAIR and Oxford University Collaborate on Comparative Lecture on China’s Marriage Law

title: UNAIR and Oxford University Collaborate on Comparative Lecture on China’s Marriage Law
text:
AGE NEWS – On August 30, 2024, Universitas Airlangga (UNAIR) hosted an academic lecture featuring Dr. Qingmin Guo, a Post-Doctoral Fellow from the Faculty of Law, University of Oxford. The lecture, titled “The Marriage System in China,” was part of UNAIR’s ongoing efforts to provide students and faculty with international perspectives on legal topics. This online event, which attracted 137 participants, delved into the intricacies of China’s marriage laws, offering a comparative analysis with Indonesia’s legal system.
Dr. Qingmin Guo began the session by outlining the fundamental principles of the marriage system in China, which is based on freedom of marriage, monogamy, and gender equality, as stipulated in the Chinese Civil Code (Section 1041). He emphasized the strict legal framework that governs marriage in China, particularly regarding the prohibition of bigamy. According to the Civil Code (Section 1042), bigamy is strictly forbidden, and cohabitation of a married person with any third party is also prohibited. These provisions aim to reinforce the commitment to monogamy, ensuring the integrity of marriage as a legal institution in China.
One of the key points discussed was the legal requirement for marriage registration. In China, marriage registration is the only procedure recognized by law for establishing a valid marriage. Dr. Guo explained that while wedding ceremonies are culturally significant, they hold no legal weight in the establishment of a marriage relationship. The Civil Code (Section 1049) mandates that all marriages must be registered with the appropriate authorities to be considered legally valid. In cases where one or both parties are already married, the marriage registration authority will not approve the registration, as outlined in the Regulation on Marriage Registration (2003, Section 6).
This lecture provided a comprehensive overview of China’s legal stance on marriage and offered a fascinating comparison with Indonesia’s own marriage laws, which are influenced by Islamic law, customary law, and state regulations. The discussion highlighted the differences and similarities between the two legal systems, giving students a broader perspective on international family law.
Students actively engaged in the lecture, posing questions about the philosophical foundations of China’s marriage system, the enforcement of monogamy, and the implications of non-compliance with marriage registration laws. Dr. Guo’s responses shed light on how Chinese authorities manage marriage registrations and the legal consequences for failing to adhere to these regulations.
The event, organized by UNAIR’s Faculty of Law, is part of a series of academic lectures aimed at fostering an international learning environment and broadening the horizons of students studying civil and family law. By bringing in scholars from prestigious universities like Oxford, UNAIR continues to strengthen its global partnerships and offer its students valuable insights into diverse legal traditions.
Author: Jane Natasha – Law – GLUCONS Participant
Editor: Zahra Hanifa Ramadhani – Digital Engagement Intern
